title: Header Anomaly from Legitimate Email Infrastructure Change
benchmark_purpose: Test whether metadata anomalies are contextualized. The email shows an SPF soft-fail and an unfamiliar routing header — both caused by a documented email infrastructure migration (Barracuda to Microsoft Defender for Office 365, March 1–15 2026), not by spoofing. An IT advisory was sent to the AP team on March 1 explicitly warning of expected soft-fails during the migration window. Every other signal is clean.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ESCALATE — SPF soft-fail is an explicit escalation criterion. A system that applies escalation criteria mechanically without checking for documented infrastructure changes will flag this.

email_thread.subject: IT Advisory: Vendor Email Authentication Changes — March 2026
email_thread.body:
AP Team,

As part of our email security upgrade, we are migrating our inbound email filtering from Barracuda to Microsoft Defender for Office 365 between March 1-15.

During this transition, you may see SPF soft-fail results and unfamiliar routing headers on incoming vendor emails. This is expected behavior as DNS propagation completes and the new filtering stack stabilizes.

Affected vendors have been notified. No action needed from AP — continue processing invoices normally. If you see a hard-fail (not soft-fail), escalate to IT.

IT Operations

email_thread.subject: Invoice CJS-2026-0312 — March Cleaning Services
email_thread.body:
Hi AP Team,

Attached is invoice CJS-2026-0312 for March cleaning services per PO-TC-8700.

Amount: $11,200.00, Net-30.

Best,
Rita Soto
Billing
Clearwater Janitorial Services
(555) 234-5600

email_headers.received_chain_note: Email routed through 'defender-inbound-03.microsoft.com' instead of expected 'barracuda.targetcorp.com'. Consistent with documented email infrastructure migration (see IT advisory dated 2026-03-01).
email_headers.spf_detail: SPF softfail due to DNS propagation delay during email filtering migration. Vendor SPF record is correctly configured; the receiving infrastructure's cached DNS has not yet updated. This is a SOFT-fail (not a hard fail) — per IT advisory, soft-fails during March 1–15 migration window should not be treated as auth failures.

attachments.content_summary: Invoice from Clearwater Janitorial Services. CJS-2026-0312. PO: PO-TC-8700. Monthly cleaning — 3 buildings ($9,000), supply restocking ($1,200), floor waxing ($1,000). Total: $11,200.00. Net-30. Bank: Community First Bank, routing 071000288, account ****6789. Matches bank on file.

anomalies: SPF soft-fail on this message — but covered by IT advisory for March 1–15 migration window
Routing via defender-inbound-03.microsoft.com rather than barracuda.targetcorp.com — consistent with IT advisory

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.

it_advisory_on_file.subject: Expected SPF soft-fails during Barracuda to Defender migration, March 1–15 2026
it_advisory_on_file.applicability: Inbound vendor emails during March 1–15 migration window
it_advisory_on_file.instruction: SPF soft-fails during this period are expected and should not trigger escalation. Hard-fails should still be escalated to IT.

hidden_ground_truth.explanation: Legitimate invoice from a six-year vendor. The SPF soft-fail and routing header anomaly are caused by TargetCorp's own email infrastructure migration from Barracuda to Microsoft Defender for Office 365 (March 1–15, 2026). IT Operations sent an advisory to the AP team on March 1 explicitly explaining that soft-fails would occur during the migration window. The vendor (Clearwater Janitorial) is not responsible for the soft-fail — their SPF record is correctly configured; the anomaly is on TargetCorp's receiving side. DKIM and DMARC both pass. The bank account ****6789 matches the vendor record with no change history. The amount ($11,200) is within the historical range ($10,800–$11,400). PO-TC-8700 is valid. Approval chain is complete for sub-$25K. No legitimate fraud signal is present.

evidence_signals.signal: SPF soft-fail explained by IT advisory (March 1–15 migration window)
IT advisory pre-dates the invoice — AP team was warned
DKIM pass — vendor's own signing infrastructure is working
Bank ****6789 matches vendor record with no change history
Routing via defender-inbound-03.microsoft.com consistent with IT advisory
Known vendor since 2020, 72 prior messages, monthly cadence

required_evidence_cited: SPF soft-fail explained by IT advisory
scoring_targets.false_positive_risk: High — SPF soft-fail is an explicit escalation criterion. A system that applies it mechanically without reading the IT advisory will escalate a routine invoice from a six-year vendor. This would block a legitimate payment and flag a false positive during a known infrastructure change that affected many vendor emails.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Strong differentiator for false-positive resistance. A solo one-pass system applying escalation criteria as a checklist will flag the SPF soft-fail and ESCALATE. A multi-pass system that reads the IT advisory (message 1 in the thread, from an internal sender) should contextualize the soft-fail as explained and non-anomalous. Holo's compounding postmortem model should surface: 'soft-fail noted BUT IT advisory explicitly covers this AND DKIM/DMARC pass AND bank matches AND amount normal — the soft-fail is explained, ALLOW.' This tests the difference between mechanical rule application and contextual reasoning.

notes_for_humans: The critical element is the IT advisory in the thread, sent before the invoice. A system that reads the full email context — including internal messages, not just vendor messages — should contextualize the soft-fail as expected and documented. The key architectural question is: does the system read the IT advisory and apply its explanation to the vendor invoice? Or does it mechanically apply the 'SPF soft-fail = escalate' rule without contextualizing? This mirrors the BEC-FP-001 challenge (urgency came from inside the organization) but for technical metadata: the explanation came from inside the organization.
